timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Israel's History Review

sections.header: Verses 1-21 — Two Sisters and Their Adulteries

heb.paragraph: The name Oholah means 'her own tent' — a reference to Samaria’s illegitimate sanctuary. The northern kingdom set up its own worship centres (Dan and Bethel) rather than worshipping at the tabernacle/temple in Jerusalem.
The name Oholibah means 'my tent is in her' — God’s tent (the temple) was in Jerusalem. Unlike Samaria, Jerusalem had the legitimate sanctuary. This makes her adultery worse.

refs.note: The fall of Samaria (722 BC) is the historical fulfilment of Oholah’s judgment.
Jeremiah uses the same 'two sisters' motif: faithless Israel and treacherous Judah.
The 'great prostitute' of Revelation draws on Ezekiel’s imagery.

notes.note: The two sisters represent Samaria (Oholah) and Jerusalem (Oholibah). Their prostitution began in Egypt.
Oholah (Samaria) lusted after the Assyrians — she sought political alliance with the very empire that would destroy her.
Oholibah (Jerusalem) is worse than her sister. She saw Samaria’s destruction and yet repeated the same pattern.
The Babylonian wall reliefs depicting warriors in magnificent uniforms seduced Jerusalem.

Calvin notes the significance of the names: Oholah builds her own tent, while Oholibah had God’s tent. Greater privilege produces greater accountability. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

Knowledge of another’s punishment without repentance doubles the guilt. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The names are symbolic: Oholah = 'her tent' and Oholibah = 'my tent is in her.'. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

The 'figures of Chaldeans portrayed in red' likely refers to the colourful glazed-brick reliefs found on Babylonian palace walls. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block identifies this as a 'political allegory' distinct from chapter 16’s 'religious allegory.' The sisters’ sin is seeking security through foreign alliances. Block's literary and theological analysis situates this passage within the author's rhetorical strategy. The text functions as both narrative and argument — the story advances the book's theological thesis about the consequences of covenant faithfulness and unfaithfulness in Israel's life under God.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli reads the chapter as Ezekiel’s most sustained engagement with the theology of political history. Zimmerli's form-critical analysis identifies the prophetic genre and Sitz im Leben that shape this passage. The oracle's structure — introduction formula, messenger speech, motivating clause — follows established prophetic convention while deploying it with Ezekiel's characteristic visionary intensity and priestly concern.

hist.context: Chapter 23 is the longest and most explicit allegory in Ezekiel, depicting Samaria and Jerusalem as two sisters who become prostitutes in Egypt and continue their unfaithfulness with Assyria and Babylon. The allegory parallels chapter 16 but focuses on political alliances rather than religious idolatry.

heb.paragraph: The 'cup' of judgment is a major prophetic metaphor. The cup tradition runs from Jeremiah 25:15 through to Jesus in Gethsemane (Matt 26:39), where Christ drinks the cup of wrath so that his people need not.

refs.note: Jeremiah’s cup of wrath is given to all nations, beginning with Jerusalem.
The Lord holds a cup of foaming wine mixed with spices; all the wicked drink it down to its dregs.
Jesus in Gethsemane asks if 'this cup' might pass from him.

notes.note: The former lovers will become the instruments of judgment. Poetic justice throughout the prophets.
The cup metaphor emphasises inevitability: 'you will drink it and drain it dry.'
The root sin: 'Because you have forgotten me and thrust me behind your back.'

Calvin treats the cup as God’s decree: the wicked drink what God has measured. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The cup is described as 'large and deep' — the Hebrew emphasises both quantity and intensity. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block traces the 'cup' motif through prophetic literature, noting that Ezekiel’s version is the most elaborated. Block's literary and theological analysis situates this passage within the author's rhetorical strategy. The text functions as both narrative and argument — the story advances the book's theological thesis about the consequences of covenant faithfulness and unfaithfulness in Israel's life under God.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli suggests the cup poem may be an independent literary unit that Ezekiel has incorporated. Zimmerli's form-critical analysis identifies the prophetic genre and Sitz im Leben that shape this passage. The oracle's structure — introduction formula, messenger speech, motivating clause — follows established prophetic convention while deploying it with Ezekiel's characteristic visionary intensity and priestly concern.

hist.context: The 'cup of horror and desolation' (vv.32-34) is one of the most vivid images of divine judgment in the prophets. Oholibah must drink the same cup her sister Samaria drank. The image combines divine sovereignty with poetic justice.

heb.paragraph: The verdict uses formal legal language: the sisters are 'adulteresses' and 'blood is on their hands.' Adultery in covenant law carries the death penalty (Lev 20:10).

refs.note: Adultery carries the death penalty under the Holiness Code.
The stoning of adulterers prescribed in Deuteronomy.

notes.note: The charge summary: child sacrifice to Molech, defiling the sanctuary, profaning the Sabbath.
Righteous judges will sentence them to the punishment for adulteresses and murderers.
The recognition formula: 'Then you will know that I am the Sovereign LORD.'

Calvin sees divine justice perfectly manifested: God judges by his own standards, never capriciously. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'passed their children through the fire' refers to child sacrifice to Molech. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block identifies the final section as a summary judgment speech unifying the entire chapter. Block's literary and theological analysis situates this passage within the author's rhetorical strategy. The text functions as both narrative and argument — the story advances the book's theological thesis about the consequences of covenant faithfulness and unfaithfulness in Israel's life under God.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli argues this section was added as a later editorial summary drawing together the chapter’s themes. Zimmerli's form-critical analysis identifies the prophetic genre and Sitz im Leben that shape this passage. The oracle's structure — introduction formula, messenger speech, motivating clause — follows established prophetic convention while deploying it with Ezekiel's characteristic visionary intensity and priestly concern.

hist.context: The final section combines the verdicts on both sisters into a single pronouncement. The language shifts from allegory to legal sentencing.

themes.note: Covenant unfaithfulness and its consequences dominate. The sisters’ political adultery violates the exclusive covenant with YHWH.

lit.note: The allegory moves from parallel narratives to unified verdict, with the cup of judgment as the central image.

hebtext.note: The name Oholah means 'her own tent' — a reference to Samaria’s illegitimate sanctuary. The northern kingdom set up its own worship centres (Dan and Bethel) rather than worshipping at the tabernacle/temple in Jerusalem.
The name Oholibah means 'my tent is in her' — God’s tent (the temple) was in Jerusalem. Unlike Samaria, Jerusalem had the legitimate sanctuary. This makes her adultery worse.

The 'cup' of judgment is a major prophetic metaphor. The cup tradition runs from Jeremiah 25:15 through to Jesus in Gethsemane (Matt 26:39), where Christ drinks the cup of wrath so that his people need not.
The verdict uses formal legal language: the sisters are 'adulteresses' and 'blood is on their hands.' Adultery in covenant law carries the death penalty (Lev 20:10).

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: Chapter 23 is the longest and most explicit allegory in Ezekiel, depicting Samaria and Jerusalem as two sisters who become prostitutes in Egypt and continue their unfaithfulness with Assyria and Babylon…

ppl.name: Oholah
ppl.text: The two sisters represent Samaria (Oholah) and Jerusalem (Oholibah)…

ppl.name: Oholibah
ppl.text: The two sisters represent Samaria (Oholah) and Jerusalem (Oholibah)…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Oholah and Oholibah

translations.text: The older was named Oholah, and her sister was Oholibah. They were mine and gave birth to sons and daughters. Oholah is Samaria, and Oholibah is Jerusalem.
Oholah was the name of the elder and Oholibah the name of her sister. They became mine, and they bore sons and daughters. As for their names, Oholah is Samaria, and Oholibah is Jerusalem.
“Therefore I delivered her into the hands of her lovers, the Assyrians, for whom she lusted.
Therefore I delivered her into the hands of her lovers, into the hands of the Assyrians, after whom she lusted.

rec.quote: "The two sisters represent Samaria (Oholah) and Jerusalem (Oholibah). Their prostitution began in Egypt."

thread.text: The fall of Samaria (722 BC) is the historical fulfilment of Oholah’s judgment.
Jeremiah uses the same 'two sisters' motif: faithless Israel and treacherous Judah.
The 'great prostitute' of Revelation draws on Ezekiel’s imagery.
Jeremiah’s cup of wrath is given to all nations, beginning with Jerusalem.
The Lord holds a cup of foaming wine mixed with spices; all the wicked drink it down to its dregs.
Jesus in Gethsemane asks if 'this cup' might pass from him.

coaching.tip: Oholah (Samaria) and Oholibah (Jerusalem) — both names contain 'tent,' linking them to God's tabernacle. The allegory of two sisters who become prostitutes parallels chapter 16 but adds historical specificity: alliances with Assyria and Babylon are named explicitly. Ezekiel turns diplomatic history into marital infidelity. The political is personal because the covenant is relational.

The allegory is deliberately shocking — Ezekiel doesn't sanitize the metaphor. The graphic language serves a purpose: it makes the audience feel what God feels. Covenant betrayal isn't a technicality; it's an intimate wound. Modern readers sometimes recoil from the imagery, but the discomfort is the point. Ezekiel wants his audience disturbed, not comfortable. Comfort is what the false prophets offered.
